stories.headline: Update: US Troop Withdrawal from Germany Draws NATO Alarm, Bipartisan Senate Concern
stories.summary:
Germany's Defense Minister Boris Pistorius said Friday that the US decision to withdraw 5,000 troops from Germany was "foreseeable," as NATO sought clarification from Washington on the scope and timeline of the pullback. Trump, when asked about the cuts Saturday night, said the US would cut "way down" and "a lot further than 5,000," without providing specifics. The withdrawal follows a public feud between Trump and German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, who had said the US was "humiliated" by Iranian negotiators during ongoing war talks.

NATO spokeswoman Allison Hart said the alliance was "working with the US to understand the details," while Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk warned that "the greatest threat to the transatlantic community are not its external enemies, but the ongoing disintegration of our alliance." Trump has also floated troop reductions in Italy and Spain.

The announcement drew rare bipartisan pushback from within Trump's own party. Senate Armed Services Committee Chairman Roger Wicker and House Armed Services Committee Chairman Mike Rogers said they were "very concerned" and argued it is in the US interest to maintain a strong deterrent in Europe rather than withdraw forces from the continent.

Germany currently hosts more than 36,000 active duty US troops, by far the largest American military presence in Europe. Pistorius noted that Germany has dramatically increased its defense spending under the Merz government, with expenditure projected to reach 3.1% of GDP — well above NATO's 2% target — and said Berlin would work more closely with European allies on continental security going forward.

stories.headline: Golden Tempo Wins Kentucky Derby at 23-1 Odds; Cherie DeVaux Becomes First Woman to Train Derby Winner
stories.summary:
Golden Tempo captured the 152nd Kentucky Derby on Saturday at Churchill Downs in Louisville, Kentucky, winning at longshot odds of 23-1 and upsetting the favored Renegade. The victory delivered a historic milestone: trainer Cherie DeVaux became the first woman to train the winner of the Kentucky Derby, the opening leg of thoroughbred racing's Triple Crown.

DeVaux's win breaks a barrier in a sport long dominated by male trainers at the sport's highest level. The Derby, held annually on the first Saturday in May, is one of the most-watched sporting events in the United States and the most prestigious race in American thoroughbred history.

Golden Tempo's upset adds to a string of longshot winners in recent Derby history and sets the stage for the Preakness Stakes, the second leg of the Triple Crown, in the coming weeks.

stories.headline: Clandestine Network Smuggles Starlink Terminals into Iran During Two-Month Internet Blackout
stories.summary:
Iran has been under one of the longest-running national internet shutdowns ever recorded — more than two months — following US and Israeli airstrikes in late February. A clandestine network of activists outside Iran has been smuggling SpaceX Starlink satellite terminals across the border to give Iranians access to uncensored internet, according to a BBC News investigation. One participant, identified only as Sahand, says he has sent a dozen terminals into Iran since January and is actively seeking ways to send more.

The Iranian government shut down the internet citing security concerns, saying it aims to prevent espionage and cyber-attacks during wartime. Last year, Iran passed legislation making the use, purchase, or sale of Starlink devices punishable by up to two years in prison, with up to ten years for distributing more than ten devices. State media has reported multiple arrests, including four people detained last month for importing satellite internet equipment.

The human rights organization Witness estimated in January that at least 50,000 Starlink terminals are already inside Iran, a number activists believe has risen since. Multiple people can share a single terminal, making each device a significant resource for communities cut off from outside information. Sahand, who spoke to the BBC outside Iran, said he fears reprisals against family members and contacts inside the country if he is identified by the regime.

The story underscores the broader humanitarian dimension of Iran's information blackout, which preceded the current shutdown by a previous cutoff in January imposed during a regime crackdown on nationwide protests. US-based human rights monitors report more than 6,500 protesters were killed and 53,000 arrested during that period. SpaceX did not respond to the BBC's request for comment.

stories.headline: U.S. Warns Shipping Firms of Sanctions for Paying Iran Strait of Hormuz Tolls
stories.summary:
The U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control issued a formal alert Friday warning shipping companies worldwide that paying Iran for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz could expose them to American sanctions. The warning covers not only U.S. firms but also non-U.S. persons whose payments could draw in American financial institutions or insurers, expanding the potential reach of enforcement. The Treasury Department simultaneously sanctioned three Iranian foreign currency exchange houses, with Secretary Scott Bessent vowing to "relentlessly target" Iran's ability to generate and move funds.

The Strait of Hormuz has seen traffic collapse from roughly 3,000 ships per month to a handful per day since Iran began restricting passage after the U.S.-Israel military strike on Iran in late February. Iran has defended the toll collections as sovereign right, with its parliament claiming initial revenues have been deposited with the Central Bank. The U.S. has also maintained a naval blockade since April 13, with Central Command reporting that 45 commercial vessels have been turned back.

The strait is a critical chokepoint for global oil, food, medicine, and technology supplies. The UN refugee agency warned Friday that closure of key maritime routes is forcing the use of longer, more dangerous overland corridors. The escalating confrontation raises fears of broader disruption to global energy markets and supply chains, coming on top of existing trade tensions between the U.S. and major economies.

stories.headline: Taiwan's President Completes Eswatini Visit After China Blocked Earlier Trip
stories.summary:
Taiwan President Lai Ching-te arrived in Eswatini on Saturday, completing a diplomatic visit that had been postponed after China pressured African nations to deny overflight rights to his aircraft. The trip — unannounced in advance — included a bilateral meeting with King Mswati III and signing of a customs agreement, according to Taiwan's presidential office. Lai said Taiwan "will never be deterred by external pressures."

The original visit, planned for late April to mark the 40th anniversary of King Mswati III's accession, was suspended after Seychelles, Mauritius, and Madagascar revoked flight permits for Lai's aircraft under what Taiwan described as Chinese diplomatic pressure. Beijing responded to Saturday's completed visit by calling it a "stowaway-style escape farce" and urging Eswatini to "see clearly the general trend of history," while also calling Lai "an international laughing stock."

Eswatini is Taiwan's only remaining diplomatic ally on the African continent and one of just 12 states globally that maintain formal relations with Taipei. The episode underscores the intensifying pressure China is exerting on Taiwan's shrinking diplomatic footprint. Notably, China announced this week it was scrapping tariffs for all African nations except Eswatini, a pointed economic signal tied directly to the kingdom's relationship with Taiwan.

stories.headline: Pentagon Signs AI Deals With Google, Microsoft, OpenAI and Four Other Firms for Classified Military Systems
stories.summary:
The Department of Defense announced Friday it has reached agreements with seven technology companies — Google, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services, Nvidia, OpenAI, Reflection, and SpaceX — to integrate artificial intelligence into classified military networks. The Pentagon said the AI capabilities will "augment warfighter decision-making in complex operational environments," including faster target identification and management of weapons maintenance and supply chains.

Notably absent from the contracts is AI firm Anthropic, which has been in a public dispute and legal fight with the Trump administration over the ethics and safety of deploying AI in warfare. One of the contracting companies said its agreement requires human oversight in certain situations, but the scope and nature of human control across the other contracts was not specified. Defense Department use of AI has accelerated sharply in recent years.

The deals have drawn scrutiny from civil liberties groups and AI researchers who warn the technology could erode privacy protections or enable machines to select battlefield targets with insufficient human oversight. Concerns intensified following reports that U.S. tech giants quietly provided AI targeting support to Israel during its military campaign in Gaza and Lebanon, where civilian casualties were high. Experts say the fundamental questions about appropriate levels of human involvement, risk tolerance, and operator training remain unresolved even as the military moves rapidly to deploy the tools.

stories.headline: Somali Pirates Hijack Second Oil Tanker in Two Weeks off Yemen Coast
stories.summary:
Somali pirates seized the oil tanker MT Eureka in the Gulf of Aden early Saturday, the second successful tanker hijacking in a 10-day period and the fourth in two weeks, according to Yemeni coastguard officials and three Puntland regional security officials who spoke with the BBC. The vessel, sailing under a Togolese flag, was overrun at 5 a.m. local time near the port of Qana and was being steered toward Somali waters. A second incident occurred separately, with armed persons on a skiff approaching a bulk carrier near Al-Mukala, Yemen.

The surge follows the April 22 hijacking of the Honor 25, which was carrying 18,500 barrels of oil bound for Mogadishu. Security officials say piracy is expanding along Somalia's entire coastline — the longest in mainland Africa — with one official warning that "the on-going crisis with the pirates is much worse than many realize."

Somali piracy, which had declined sharply since 2011, began resurging in late 2023 when Houthi rebel attacks in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden drew international naval forces away from anti-piracy operations, creating a security vacuum exploited by armed coastal groups. The European Union Naval Force and Somali authorities had not publicly addressed Saturday's hijacking as of the time of reporting.

stories.headline: Trump Signs Executive Order Imposing New Sanctions on Cuba, Threatens Military Presence
stories.summary:
President Trump signed an executive order Friday targeting Cuban officials in the energy, defense, financial, and security sectors, as well as individuals the administration alleges have committed human rights abuses or corruption. The move represents a further tightening of U.S. foreign policy toward the communist-governed island, which lies roughly 90 miles from Florida.

In a remark to an audience in Florida Friday night, Trump escalated his rhetoric significantly, saying the U.S. would be "taking over" Cuba "almost immediately" and suggesting a U.S. aircraft carrier could be deployed offshore as a show of force. Cuban Foreign Minister Bruno Rodriguez condemned the sanctions as "illegal and abusive," saying the measures violated the United Nations Charter and amounted to "collective punishment against the Cuban people."

The new sanctions come despite Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel confirming in March that Havana and Washington were engaged in negotiations over bilateral relations. A U.S. oil blockade has already caused widespread blackouts and fuel shortages across the island, affecting hospitals, public transport, and schools, with only one Russian oil tanker reaching Cuba since the blockade was imposed. Trump has also threatened tariffs on any country that supplies oil to Cuba.

The announcement coincided with May Day demonstrations outside the U.S. Embassy in Havana, where protesters denounced the blockade. The episode underscores deepening U.S.-Cuba tensions at a moment when diplomatic contacts had appeared to offer a narrow opening.

stories.headline: Ukrainian Drone Strike Hits Russian Oil Terminal at Black Sea Port of Tuapse
stories.summary:
A fire at a marine oil terminal in the Russian Black Sea port of Tuapse was extinguished Saturday after Ukrainian forces struck the facility with a drone attack on Friday, according to emergency authorities in Russia's Krasnodar region, who posted the update on Telegram. The Tuapse terminal is one of Russia's significant Black Sea energy export hubs.

The strike is part of an ongoing Ukrainian campaign targeting Russian energy and logistics infrastructure, which Kyiv has pursued as a means of degrading Moscow's revenue base and supply chains. Russia has not publicly announced any casualties or detailed damage assessments.

The attack comes amid continued active military operations along multiple fronts in the more than two-year conflict. Ukrainian drone and missile strikes on Russian territory, including energy infrastructure, have become a consistent feature of the war's strategic landscape.

stories.headline: California to Allow Police to Ticket Autonomous Vehicle Manufacturers for Traffic Violations
stories.summary:
California's Department of Motor Vehicles announced new regulations that will, for the first time, allow police to issue formal citations to autonomous vehicle manufacturers when their driverless cars commit moving violations. The rules take effect July 1 and are described by the DMV as "the most comprehensive AV regulations in the nation."

Under the new framework, officers can issue a "notice of AV noncompliance" directly to the manufacturer rather than a driver. Companies will also be required to respond to calls from police and emergency officials within 30 seconds and face penalties if their vehicles enter active emergency zones. The rules stem from a broader 2024 state law imposing deeper oversight of autonomous vehicle technology.

The regulations address a gap that had left law enforcement unable to hold driverless vehicles accountable for violations. In one documented incident last September, San Bruno police watched a Waymo vehicle make an illegal U-turn directly in front of them but could not issue a ticket because there was no driver present. A major San Francisco blackout last December left multiple Waymo vehicles stalled in busy intersections, compounding traffic problems, and the San Francisco Fire Department has repeatedly complained about robotaxis obstructing emergency responses.

Waymo is the primary operator of fully autonomous robotaxis in the San Francisco Bay Area and Los Angeles County, though Tesla and other companies also hold permits to test autonomous vehicles in California cities. The state's action comes as questions about AV accountability and public safety grow alongside the rapid expansion of the technology.

stories.headline: Update: Missing Oscar Found in Frankfurt After TSA Forced Anti-Putin Documentary Filmmaker to Check It
stories.summary:
Lufthansa confirmed Saturday that an Academy Award statue belonging to Pavel Talankin, co-director of the Best Feature Documentary winner "Mr Nobody Against Putin," has been located in Frankfurt after going missing during a checked-bag incident at JFK Airport. Talankin had carried the Oscar in his carry-on bag for a flight to Germany, but TSA stopped him at JFK, saying the statuette could be used as a weapon and requiring him to check it. When he landed in Frankfurt, the Oscar was gone.

Lufthansa told the BBC: "The Oscar statue has now been located and is safely in our care in Frankfurt," and said the airline was in direct contact with Talankin to arrange its return "as quickly as possible." The airline added that an internal review of the circumstances was ongoing and expressed regret for the inconvenience.

The documentary's executive producer Robin Hessman said Talankin had flown multiple times previously with both his Oscar and his BAFTA without incident, and disputed the necessity of forcing him to check the award. "This wouldn't have happened to Leonardo DiCaprio," she said. The BBC has contacted the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences and the TSA for comment.

"Mr Nobody Against Putin" documents the intensification of war propaganda in a Russian school following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. Talankin is now exiled from Russia for his safety; Russia has banned the documentary from streaming platforms on grounds that it "propagates extremism and terrorism."

stories.headline: Update: UK Terror Threat Raised to Severe as PM Starmer Calls for Tougher Action on Antisemitic Protest Chants
stories.summary:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer called Saturday for tougher law enforcement action against certain chants at pro-Palestinian protests, saying some marches may need to be banned, as concerns mounted over the safety of British Jews following the stabbing of two Jewish men in London's Golders Green neighborhood. A 45-year-old man was charged Friday with attempted murder in connection with the Wednesday attack, which police classified as an act of terrorism.

The UK's terror threat level was raised from "substantial" to "severe" — the second-highest on a five-point scale — meaning intelligence agencies consider an attack highly likely within six months. Authorities said the change reflected not only the Golders Green attack but also a broader increase in danger from Islamist and extreme right-wing threats. Metropolitan Police Commissioner Mark Rowley warned that British Jews face "their greatest ever threat" and blamed social media for mainstreaming antisemitism.

Starmer singled out the chant "globalize the intifada" as warranting enforcement action, while affirming that the right to protest must be protected. He suggested repeated pro-Palestinian marches have had a "cumulative effect" contributing to a rise in antisemitic incidents. The Community Security Trust recorded 3,700 antisemitic incidents across the UK in 2025, up from 1,662 in 2022.

The development follows a string of recent incidents including arson attacks on synagogues and Jewish sites in London. Rowley described Jews as being "on everybody's list" of extremist targets, citing threats from far-right, far-left, Islamist, and state-linked Iranian-related groups. Community members told CBS News they now live in constant fear of attack.

stories.headline: Update: Aung San Suu Kyi Moved to House Arrest After Five Years in Military Detention, Myanmar Junta Says
stories.summary:
Myanmar's military junta announced Saturday that former leader Aung San Suu Kyi, 80, has been transferred to house arrest after more than five years in military detention following the February 2021 coup. State media published a statement from coup leader Min Aung Hlaing saying her remaining sentence had been "commuted to be served at the designated residence," though the image released alongside the announcement was taken in 2022.

Suu Kyi's son, Kim Aris, expressed deep skepticism, saying he had no proof his mother was even alive and that the photograph was "meaningless" given its age. Her legal team told Reuters they had received no direct notification of the transfer. Aris said he would not believe the announcement until he was allowed direct communication or an independent party could verify her condition and whereabouts.

Analysts suggested the move is part of a broader public relations effort by the military junta to reduce international isolation. Coup leader Min Aung Hlaing held elections earlier this year restoring a nominally democratic government while keeping military leaders in power, and has achieved recent battlefield gains against armed opposition groups. Former adviser Sean Turnell told the BBC the junta is "trying to convince the world that it's a legitimate government."

Suu Kyi came to power in 2015 following democratic reforms and spent decades as a pro-democracy activist before the coup. She had previously spent more than 15 years under house arrest during earlier periods of military rule. Her 33-year sentence on charges widely dismissed as fabricated has been reduced several times. Her lawyers have not seen her in more than three years; her family has had no contact for over two years.

stories.headline: Rats and Disease Infest Gaza Displacement Camps, Affecting 1.45 Million People as Humanitarian Crisis Deepens
stories.summary:
A severe rodent and pest infestation has taken hold across displacement camps in Gaza, affecting an estimated 1.45 million people, according to a survey cited by UN agencies. Aid workers are calling the situation a public health crisis, with rats, urban weasels and other pests biting children, the sick and elderly, and contaminating food and water supplies. Raw sewage running through overcrowded campsites and accumulating piles of rubbish have created ideal breeding conditions for rodents.

The BBC documented multiple cases of injury, including a four-year-old girl bitten on the hand by a weasel while sleeping, who subsequently suffered days of fever and vomiting after receiving a tetanus injection. A diabetic grandmother described having parts of her toes bitten off due to nerve damage that prevented her from feeling the attacks. Social media footage has circulated widely showing rodents attacking newborns, the sick, and elderly.

The World Health Organization's local representative Dr. Reinhilde Van De Weert called the infestations "the predictable consequence of a collapsed living environment." More than six months after the U.S. brokered a Gaza ceasefire deal, no reconstruction has taken place and Palestinian officials say 200,000 caravans needed as temporary housing have not been delivered. Israel's Cogat body, which controls Gaza's crossings, said it was working with international organizations to address sanitation needs.

Regular deadly airstrikes continue, with Israel saying it is targeting Hamas. Hamas, which triggered the conflict with its October 2023 assault on Israel and mass hostage-taking, has not committed to disarming. Displaced families report keeping vigil through the night to protect children from animal attacks, with many saying sleep has become impossible.

stories.headline: Update: Suspect in Trump Press Dinner Shooting — Key Details Remain Disputed
stories.summary:
Nearly a week after Cole Tomas Allen, 31, allegedly attempted to attack President Trump at the White House Correspondents' Association dinner at the Washington Hilton, investigators and prosecutors are still unable to confirm whether Allen actually shot a Secret Service officer. Trump and senior officials initially stated that an officer was shot and survived due to a bulletproof vest, but court documents filed by government attorneys do not explicitly allege the suspect fired the shot that struck the agent.

Acting U.S. Attorney General Todd Blanche walked back his own Sunday statement on the matter by Monday, saying ballistics were still being analyzed. Court filings describe Allen charging through a security magnetometer with a long gun and a Secret Service officer being shot once in the chest, but leave open the question of who fired the round. Allen was found armed with a semi-automatic handgun, a pump-action shotgun, and three knives.

Legal analysts warn that contradictory public statements by law enforcement could give defense attorneys grounds to challenge the government's case. The Justice Department, Secret Service, and U.S. Attorney's office have declined to provide further comment. The discrepancy between the president's public assertions and the formal legal record has drawn scrutiny as the investigation continues.

stories.headline: Trump Announces 25% Tariffs on EU Cars and Trucks, Escalating Trade Tensions
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump announced Friday that he will raise tariffs on cars and trucks imported from the European Union to 25%, escalating an already strained transatlantic trade relationship. Trump claimed the EU was not adhering to a previously negotiated trade deal struck at his Turnberry golf course in Scotland, though he declined to specify how the EU had failed to comply when pressed by reporters.

The European Commission pushed back, stating the EU was implementing the agreement in line with standard legislative practice and had kept U.S. officials fully informed. The Commission said it would keep "all options open" to protect EU interests if the U.S. took measures inconsistent with the joint statement. The automotive sector is a particularly sensitive target — car manufacturing represents a substantial share of several major European economies, including Germany and France.

The tariff hike comes after months of stalled negotiations over steel and aluminium and follows European Parliament approval of the broader trade deal in March, which included a clause allowing suspension if the U.S. was deemed to have engaged in economic coercion or threatened member states' territorial integrity. Trump urged European automakers to shift production to the United States, promising no tariff for cars built on American soil. The announcement rattled global markets and signals a potential breakdown of the Turnberry framework that had offered EU exporters temporary relief from steeper "Liberation Day" levies.

stories.headline: Fifth Circuit Restricts Mail-Order Access to Abortion Pill Mifepristone
stories.summary:
The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals issued an order Friday temporarily reinstating the requirement that mifepristone — the most commonly used abortion pill in the United States — be obtained in person rather than through mail-order pharmacy or telemedicine. The ruling overrides a lower court decision that had paused the case while the Trump administration conducts an FDA review, and stems from a lawsuit brought by the state of Louisiana.

The decision effectively suspends a 2023 FDA regulation that had permanently lifted the in-person dispensing requirement first loosened during the COVID-19 pandemic. The appeals court ruled that the FDA's action undermined Louisiana's abortion ban, writing that "every abortion facilitated by FDA's action cancels Louisiana's ban on medical abortions." The order will remain in effect as the underlying case is litigated.

Supporters of the ruling called it a significant victory. Louisiana Attorney General Liz Murrill said the Biden-era rule "facilitated the deaths of thousands of Louisiana babies," and Marjorie Dannenfelser of Susan B Anthony Pro-Life America praised it as a win for "victims and survivors of Biden's reckless mail-order abortion drug regime." Critics were sharply opposed: ACLU attorney Julia Kaye called the decision one that "defies clear science and settled law," and New York Attorney General Letitia James said abortion access — including medication abortion — remains fully legal in her state and that her office will continue to protect it. In 2024, the Supreme Court had unanimously rejected a separate effort to restrict mifepristone access, but left the door open to further legal challenges.

stories.headline: Turkey Arrests More Than 500 at May Day Rallies in Istanbul
stories.summary:
Turkish police arrested more than 500 people and deployed tear gas and water cannons against demonstrators attempting to march to Taksim Square in Istanbul on International Labour Day. The square has been sealed off to protests since 2013, and police blocked access overnight ahead of Friday's demonstrations. The CHD lawyers' group, which was present at the rallies, confirmed the arrest toll.

Among those confronted by police was Erkan Bas, president of the Turkish Workers' Party, who was part of a group in the Mecidiyekoy district that breached a police barricade before being dispersed with water cannons. Permitted rallies went ahead in other districts and cities including Kadikoy, Kartal, Izmir, Diyarbakir, and Ankara, where groups gathered at approved locations under heavy security.

President Recep Tayyip Erdogan posted a Labor Day greeting on social media, saying he congratulated workers "who earn their living through their labor." The annual crackdown on Taksim Square marches has become a flashpoint in Turkey's ongoing tensions between the government and labor and opposition movements, particularly as the country continues to face political pressure following a series of high-profile arrests of opposition figures earlier this year.
